span.p:
STEP 1 (agent to toolbox): agent managed identity
needs the Foundry user role on the project

span.p:
oauth2 — managed or BYO app reg
1st call returns CONSENT_REQUIRED (-32006)

span.p:
STEP 2 (toolbox to tool): project connection
authType decides whose identity is used

div.style: display: table-cell; white-space: nowrap; line-height: 1.5; max-width: 200px; text-align: center;
color: rgb(16, 42, 84) !important; font-weight: bold !important; display: table-cell; white-space: nowrap; line-height: 1.5; max-width: 200px; text-align: center;
color: rgb(19, 67, 29) !important; display: table; white-space: break-spaces; line-height: 1.5; max-width: 200px; text-align: center; width: 200px;

span.p: Downstream tool / MCP server / REST API

div.style: color: rgb(51, 33, 92) !important; display: table; white-space: break-spaces; line-height: 1.5; max-width: 200px; text-align: center; width: 200px;

span.p:
For oauth2 + user-entra-token (OBO):
the hosted agent forwards the caller / end-user token →
the toolbox uses THAT token to reach the tool,
so the tool acts as the real end user

div.style: color: rgb(92, 59, 0) !important; font-weight: bold !important; display: table; white-space: break-spaces; line-height: 1.5; max-width: 200px; text-align: center; width: 200px;

span.p:
Better together (hosted agent + toolbox):
the agent authenticates to the toolbox with its OWN
managed identity (the trust boundary), yet the END-USER
identity still flows through to the downstream tool —
the hosted agent does this passthrough automatically,
with no OAuth / token-exchange wiring in your agent code
